• External ventricular drain Endoscopic third ventriculostomy Ventriculoperitoneal shunt Upper end is put into lateral ventricles Lower end into Intra peritoneal cavity A shunt valve inserted between the proximal and distal catheters regulates flow through the system by opening at a predetermined pressure; the shunt valve typically incorporates a CSF reservoir, which allows for percutaneous sampling.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 705

subject: Anesthesia
question: Most common tissue of origin for brain metastases is:

explanation.detail: • Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 715

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 45-year-old male presents with personality change, gait disturbance and urinary incontinence since 2 months. A CT Brain is likely to show a mass in which lobe?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The Temporal lobe is primarily involved with processing auditory information and memory . Lesions here might lead to problems with memory , understanding language , or disturbances in hearing .
• Option B . The Parietal lobe integrates sensory information from various modalities, including spatial sense and navigation (proprioception) , the main sensory receptive area for the sense of touch . Disturbances here typically affect spatial awareness and proprioception .
• Option C . The Occipital lobe is the visual processing center of the brain . Lesions in the occipital lobe can lead to visual deficits like blindness or visual field cuts .
• Educational Objective
• A mass in the frontal lobe of the brain can present with personality changes , gait disturbances , and urinary incontinence , correlating with the functions mediated by this area of the brain .
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 714

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency room with severe chest pain and difficulty swallowing. A CT scan with water soluble contrast reveals an esophageal perforation. What is the most common etiology of esophageal perforation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Spontaneous. Spontaneous perforation of the esophagus , also known as Boerhaave syndrome , is a serious condition but not the most common cause . It occurs due to vomiting against a closed glottis , which leads to a sudden increase in esophageal pressure and typically results in perforation of the lower esophagus.
• Option B: Blunt trauma to the chest. Blunt trauma can cause esophageal perforation ; however, it is less common than iatrogenic causes related to medical procedures.
• Option D: Penetrating trauma to chest. Penetrating trauma is another possible cause of esophageal injury , but again, it is not as common as iatrogenic causes.
• Educational Objective:
• The most common cause of esophageal perforation is iatrogenic injury during instrumental procedures such as upper GI endoscopy, dilatation, and stenting .
• Iatrogenic perforation secondary to endoscopic procedures such as dilatation of strictures or achalasia is the most common cause. Esophageal perforation can occur due to instrumental procedures such as upper GI endoscopy , dilatation, and stenting. Spontaneous esophageal perforation , also known as Boerhaave syndrome , can occur when vomiting against a closed glottis , resulting in a massive increase in esophageal pressure and typically perforating the lower esophagus over the left pleura. CT with water-soluble contrast is the ideal imaging modality to diagnose esophageal perforation. Treatment options for esophageal perforation include conservative management for small perforations and surgery for septic load (Boerhaave syndrome), unstable mediastinitis, or intra-abdominal esophageal perforation.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1127

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which type of esophageal perforation requires urgent surgical exploration?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cervical: Esophageal perforation in the cervical region is rare but can occur due to trauma or surgical procedures . Depending on the size and severity of the perforation, it may require urgent surgical exploration.
• Option B. Scope induced: Esophageal perforation induced by an upper GI endoscopy, dilatation, or stenting can range from small mucosal tears to full-thickness perforations. The size and extent of the perforation will determine the treatment approach. Small perforations may be managed conservatively, while larger perforations may require surgical exploration.
• Option D.  Oesophageal cancer can perforate , and the prognosis is usually poor since it reflects the underlying advanced disease.
• Educational Objective:
• Urgent surgical exploration is most commonly required for spontaneous esophageal perforations , also known as Boerhaave syndrome , due to the typically large rupture and associated high septic load . It can occur when vomiting against a closed glottis, resulting in a massive increase in esophageal pressure and typically perforating the lower esophagus over the left pleura.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1128.

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which type of caustic injury to esophagus causes deeper tissue damage?
options: Both acid and alkali poisons cause the same extent of damage
Caustic injury does not cause tissue damage

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acid poisons cause coagulative necrosis , which results in superficial tissue damage . The extent of trauma is generally less than that caused by alkali poisons.
• Option C. Acid and alkali poisons cause different types of tissue damage, with alkali poisons causing deeper damage than acid poisons .
• Option D. Caustic injury always causes tissue damage and can lead to scarring and strictures in the esophagus, stomach, and duodenum. It is a premalignant condition for esophageal carcinoma.
• Therefore, the correct answer is B) Alkali poisons cause deeper tissue damage than acid poisons.
• Educational Objective:
• Alkali poisons cause deeper tissue damage to the esophagus compared to acid poisons due to their mechanism of causing liquefactive necrosis , which allows for deeper penetration and more extensive injury .
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1129-30

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 5-year-old child accidentally swallowed a foreign object while playing. What is the most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Food bolus . A food bolus can cause obstruction but is typically not as dangerous as other objects because it often softens and passes through the gastrointestinal tract or can be removed endoscopically without causing significant damage.
• Option B: Fish Bone . A fish bone can be sharp and potentially puncture the esophagus or other parts of the gastrointestinal tract . However, it is generally less harmful compared to corrosive or electrically charged objects because it does not cause tissue necrosis or systemic toxicity.
• Option D: Coin. A coin can become lodged in the esophagus , particularly at one of the natural constrictions , but it generally does not cause the same degree of tissue damage as a battery . The coin can often be retrieved endoscopically without significant complication.
• Educational Objective:
• The most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow is a battery due to the risk of electrical discharge , leakage of caustic substances, and the potential for rapid tissue damage and necrosis.
• Swallowing a foreign object can be a serious medical emergency, especially in children. The most common foreign objects that are swallowed are food boluses and bones . However, the most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow is a battery. The esophagus has several constrictions where foreign objects can become lodged. The three most common locations for foreign body impaction are at the cricopharynx (15cm from the incisors), at the left bronchus crossing the esophagus (25cm from the incisors), and at the diaphragmatic pleura (40cm from the incisors). The first step in managing a swallowed foreign body is to obtain a chest X-ray to determine the location of the object. If the object is in the esophagus or stomach, endoscopic retrieval may be possible . However, if the object is a battery, it must be removed as an emergency via endoscopy or colonoscopy , depending on the location of the foreign body.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1132

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 30-year-old female presents with colicky abdominal pain and bilious vomiting. She reports weight loss following a keto-diet in the past few months. On investigation, the small bowel appears normal, but a CECT of the abdomen reveals compression of the D3 segment of the duodenum due to narrowing of the angle of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). Which of the following is the best option for treatment of this condition?
options: Administration of medications to relieve pain
Feeding the patient to promote weight gain
Endoscopic intervention to widen the narrowed angle

explanation.short: Ans. C) Feeding the patient to promote weight gain
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Surgery to remove the obstruction . Surgical intervention may be considered if conservative measures fail . It is not the first line of treatment because the syndrome is often caused by a lack of mesenteric fat padding, not an actual physical obstruction that needs to be removed.
• Option B: Administration of medications to relieve pain . While pain management is an important aspect of patient care , medications alone will not address the underlying issue of the narrowed angle between the SMA and the aorta, which is causing the duodenal compression.
• Option D: Endoscopic intervention to widen the narrowed angle . Endoscopic intervention is not the standard treatment for SMA Syndrome because the problem is not within the duodenal lumen but rather external compression.
• Educational Objective:
• The initial treatment for SMA Syndrome is conservative , focusing on nutritional support to promote weight gain and increase the mesenteric fat pad, thereby relieving the compression of the duodenum.
• SMA Syndrome , also known as Wilkie Syndrome , is a rare cause of intestinal obstruction . It occurs due to the narrowing of the angle between the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and aorta, leading to compression of the third part of the duodenum (D3) .
• Sudden weight loss Anorexia nervosa Prolonged bed rest Scoliosis The clinical features of SMA Syndrome include colicky abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, and weight loss. On investigation, the small bowel appears normal, but a CECT of the abdomen may reveal compression of the D3 segment of the duodenum due to narrowing of the angle of the SMA. The initial treatment for SMA Syndrome is conservative and involves feeding the patient to promote weight gain and open the narrowed angle. This can be achieved through the placement of a naso-jejunostomy tube. If this fails, surgery may be required to bypass the duodenum and relieve the obstruction.
explanation.ref: Online article: https://doi.org/10.53347/rID-11142

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 50-year-old male patient with a history of peptic ulcer disease presents with a complaint of non-bilious vomiting. On examination, a succussion splash is heard over abdomen. CECT reveals the presence of a pyloric stricture. Which of the following is the first step for management of this condition?
options: Conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout
Surgical reconstruction using Billroth II procedure
Mikulicz stricturoplasty to relieve the obstruction

explanation.short: Ans. A) Conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Surgical reconstruction using Billroth II procedure . The Billroth II procedure is a surgical option (gastro-jejunostomy) that is generally considered after conservative management has failed.
• Option C: Endoscopic dilatation of the stricture . Endoscopic dilatation is a less invasive treatment option compared to surgery and can be effective in managing strictures . However, it is not the first-line treatment before attempting nasogastric decompression.
• Option D: Mikulicz strictureplasty to relieve the obstruction . Mikulicz strictureplasty is a surgical technique used when strictures are not amenable to endoscopic dilatation . It is considered a more advanced step if other less invasive measures fail.
• Educational Objective:
• The first step in managing a pyloric stricture is conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout to provide symptomatic relief
• Pyloric stricture is a late complication of peptic ulcer disease , which occurs due to the fibrosis and scarring that develop when the circumferential ulcers over the stomach heal. This can lead to narrowing of the stomach or gastric outlet obstruction, which usually occurs at the first part of the duodenum. Clinical features of pyloric stricture include a history of peptic ulcer disease and non-bilious vomiting . The management of pyloric stricture depends on the extent and severity of the obstruction . Initial conservative management involves nasogastric decompression and washout , which can provide symptomatic relief. Endoscopy can be used to confirm the presence of a stricture and to obtain a biopsy to rule out adenocarcinoma . If the stricture is benign, it can be treated with dilatation. If dilatation fails, surgery may be required to relieve the obstruction. Mikulicz stricturoplasty is a surgical procedure that involves opening the pylorus horizontally and closing it vertically to relieve the obstruction . This is a preferred option when the stricture is not amenable to endoscopic dilatation. Billroth II reconstruction is another surgical option that involves removal of the pylorus and reconstruction of the gastrointestinal tract to improve gastric emptying.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1169

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 60-year-old male, a known case of chronic atrial fibrillation presents with acute abdominal pain and PR bleed. Abdominal examination revealed vague tenderness and diminished bowel sounds. Which of the following is the investigation of choice for diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Serum lipase. While serum lipase can be useful in diagnosing pancreatitis , it would not be the most helpful initial test for a patient suspected of having mesenteric ischemia.
• Option B: USG abdomen. An ultrasound of the abdomen can be useful for assessing many abdominal conditions, but it may not be the best choice for evaluating blood flow or identifying mesenteric ischemia, especially in an acute setting.
• Option C: X-ray upper abdomen erect. An erect abdominal X-ray can show signs of bowel obstruction or free air under the diaphragm in cases of perforation but is not the most sensitive or specific test for mesenteric ischemia.
• Educational Objective:
• For a patient with suspected acute mesenteric ischemia , particularly in the context of chronic atrial fibrillation , the next best investigation is a CECT abdomen angiogram.
• SMA emboli may be carried from the left atrium in atrial fibrillation , the left ventricle after mural myocardial infarction , vegetations on mitral and aortic valves associated with endocarditis or an atheromatous plaque from an aortic aneurysm Investigation for mesenteric ischemia includes a CECT scan, which can determine whether the occlusion is acute or chronic and assess bowel viability or gangrene. Treatment for mesenteric ischemia depends on the extent and severity of the obstruction. Conservative management with antibiotics and bowel rest may be used in some cases. For acute occlusion, mesenteric vascular intervention is the preferred treatment . If there is evidence of gangrene, laparotomy and bowel resection may be required. Chronic mesenteric ischemia typically presents with mesenteric angina (central abdominal pain) and diarrhoea. A CECT angiogram can reveal atherosclerosis of the mesentery. Treatment for chronic mesenteric ischemia involves mesenteric angioplasty bypass.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1312-1313

subject: Anesthesia
question: What is the management protocol for enterocutaneous fistula?
options: Sepsis control and skin care, TPN, CECT, and planned repair
Antibiotics, enteral nutrition, MRI, and surgical intervention
Conservative management, low-residue diet, ultrasound, and outpatient follow-up
Bowel rest, antibiotics, barium enema, and observation

explanation.short: Ans. A) Sepsis control and skin care, TPN, CECT, and planned repair
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Antibiotics, enteral nutrition, MRI, and surgical intervention . While antibiotics and surgical intervention may be part of the management , enteral nutrition may not be feasible with a high-output fistula, and MRI is not the standard imaging modality for this condition.
• Option C: Conservative management, low-residue diet, ultrasound, and outpatient follow-up . Conservative management may be indicated in some cases , but this option lacks the aggressive approach often required for enterocutaneous fistulas, such as TPN and detailed imaging.
• Option D: Bowel rest, antibiotics, barium enema, and observation . Bowel rest and observation are part of the initial management , but merely antibiotics might not be sufficient for sepsis control , and a barium enema is not typically used for fistula assessment due to the risk of extravasation and worsening sepsis.
• Educational Objective:
• The management protocol for enterocutaneous fistula involves sepsis control, skin care, total parenteral nutrition (TPN), anatomical delineation with a CECT scan , and planned surgical repair following the SNAP protocol.
• Enterocutaneous fistula is an abnormal communication between the bowel and the skin, which can be caused by postoperative complications, Crohn's disease, GI malignancies, or radiation enteritis . The management of enterocutaneous fistula is based on the SNAP protocol . Sepsis control and skin care are essential for the management of enterocutaneous fistula, along with total parenteral nutrition (TPN) to provide adequate nutrition. Anatomical delineation, typically through a CECT scan, is necessary to identify the extent of the fistula and plan for repair. Planned repair is the ultimate goal of management, but high-output fistulas (>500ml) may not close spontaneously , and additional interventions may be required. Conservative management, a low-residue diet, and ultrasound are not effective for managing enterocutaneous fistula, and surgical intervention is required for definitive treatment.
• Principles of management of enterocutaneous fistulae (SNAP)
• S - elimination of S epsis and S kin protection
• N - N utrition – a period of parenteral nutrition may well be required
• A - A natomical assessment
• P - definitive P lanned surgery
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1315-16

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 50-year-old patient underwent surgery for colon cancer, and a transverse colostomy was created. Which of the following complications is the patient unlikely to experience?
options: Excoriation of surrounding skin is more common than ileostomy
Loop stomas are usually done for temporary indications
Loop ileostomy is easier to construct than loop transverse colostomy
Ileostomy is spouted, whereas colostomy is flushed with the skin

explanation.short: Ans. A) Excoriation of surrounding skin is more common than ileostomy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Temporary or defunctioning stomas are usually fashioned as loop stomas .
• Option C: The advantage of a loop ileostomy over a loop colostomy is the ease with which the bowel can be brought to the surface .
• Option D: An ileostomy is spouted ; a colostomy is flush because the ileostomy effluent is usually liquid , whereas colostomy effluent is usually solid . The spouts permit easier drainage of the secretions.
• Educational objective:
• It is the surgical exteriorization of a portion of the small bowel or colon . In this case, the patient underwent surgery for colon cancer, and a transverse colostomy was created. Stoma creation is indicated to protect anastomosis and for intestinal obstruction.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1313-14.

subject: Anesthesia
question:
A 30-year-old male, non-smoker presented with intermittent dysphagia for the past 3 months. Barium swallow showed the following characteristic appearance. What is the most probable diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/06/03/untitled-589.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Achalasia cardia. Achalasia cardia typically presents with a dilated esophagus and a "bird's beak" narrowing at the gastroesophageal junction. The absence of normal peristalsis in the body of the esophagus is also characteristic.
• Option B: Plummer Vinson syndrome. Plummer Vinson syndrome is associated with upper esophageal webs and iron deficiency anemia. Barium swallow may show thin web-like structures in the upper esophagus.
• Option D: Ca Esophagus. Esophageal cancer can present with various findings on a barium swallow, including irregular or nodular filling defects, luminal narrowing, and possibly evidence of tumor invasion . It does not typically present as a thin circumferential ring.
• The description of the image suggests that the most likely diagnosis is a Schatzki ring, as it is characterized by a thin circumferential ring in the distal esophagus.
• Educational Objective:
• A Schatzki ring , presenting as a thin circumferential constriction at the gastroesophageal junction on a barium swallow , is the most probable diagnosis in a patient with intermittent dysphagia.
• Schatzki ring is a benign condition There is a concentric, fibrous thickening and narrowing of the gastro-esophageal junction (GEJ), with squamous epithelium above and columnar cells below. Most common presentation - Dysphagia (if diameter < 13mm) and gastro-esophageal reflux disorder (GERD). Most common site - lower third of the esophagus. Treatment is endoscopic dilation with anti-reflux therapy
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love's Short Practice of Surgery 28th Edition pg 1147

subject: Anesthesia
question: A young schizophrenic female presents with abdominal pain and vomiting. The surgeon notices that she has scanty hair on scalp, and on palpation of abdomen there is crepitus in the stomach. She is likely to be suffering from?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Phytobezoar. Phytobezoars are composed of indigestible plant material and are typically found in patients with impaired gastric motility , often secondary to gastric surgery, and not necessarily associated with psychiatric conditions or hair ingestion.
• Option C: Ca stomach. Gastric cancer (Ca stomach) could present with abdominal pain and vomiting but would not typically present with crepitus in the stomach or be associated with the ingestion of hair.
• Option D: Gastric volvulus. Gastric volvulus presents with acute abdominal pain and may lead to vomiting, but it is unlikely to be associated with hair ingestion or crepitus on abdominal palpation unless there is associated gastric strangulation and perforation, which is a medical emergency.
• The clinical picture provided most consistently aligns with a trichobezoar , given the patient's psychiatric history and the specific finding of crepitus in the stomach on palpation.
• Educational Objective:
• In a young psychiatric patient presenting with abdominal pain, vomiting, and evidence of scanty scalp hair, along with palpable crepitus in the stomach, the likely diagnosis is a trichobezoar.
• Trichobezoar (hair balls) are unusual and are virtually exclusively found in young psychiatric patients. It is caused by the ingestion of hair, which remains undigested in the stomach. The hair ball can lead to ulceration and gastrointestinal bleeding, perforation or obstruction . The diagnosis is made easily at endoscopy. Treatment consists of removal of the bezoar, which may require open surgical treatment. Phytobezoars are made of vegetable matter and are found principally in patients who have gastric stasis, usually following gastric surgery.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love's Short Practice of Surgery 28th Edition pg 1180

subject: Anesthesia
question: All of these are a part of Borchardt’s triad seen in gastric volvulus except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Borchardt’s triad is a classical clinical presentation associated with gastric volvulus , which is a rotation of the stomach by more than 180 degrees, creating a closed-loop obstruction that can compromise the blood supply . The triad includes the following components:
• Option A: Acute epigastric pain . Patients typically present with sudden onset of severe epigastric pain due to the rotation of the stomach and potential compromise of its blood supply.
• Option C: Vomiting . Vomiting or retching is a common feature due to the obstruction of the GI tract . However, in some cases of complete volvulus, vomiting may not be possible as the rotation prevents gastric emptying.
• Option D: Inability to pass Ryle’s tube . The inability to pass a nasogastric tube (Ryle’s tube) into the stomach is due to the physical obstruction created by the volvulus , and it is a classic sign of this condition.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1180-81

subject: Anesthesia
question:
A 49 years old male presents with long-standing vague abdominal symptoms, early satiety, episodic abdominal pain, associated with vomiting. His imaging shows the following picture. What is the condition he is suffering from?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/09/16/image.png]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Gastric volvulus . Gastric volvulus is an abnormal rotation of the stomach, which would typically show a distended stomach with an abnormal orientation on imaging . It often presents acutely with severe abdominal pain and retching without vomitus.
• Option B: Ca stomach . Cancer of the stomach may present with nonspecific symptoms like weight loss, early satiety, and possibly a mass or irregularity on imaging , but it does not typically cause the acute angulation of the SMA leading to duodenal compression.
• Option D: Gastric ulcer . A gastric ulcer may present with abdominal pain and symptoms related to eating , but it would not cause the compression of the duodenum as seen in SMA syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• In a patient presenting with chronic vague abdominal symptoms, early satiety, and vomiting, along with imaging showing compression of the third part of the duodenum by the SMA, the diagnosis is likely Superior Mesenteric Artery Syndrome.
• Superior mesenteric artery (SMA) syndrome , also known as Wilkie syndrome is a rare acquired vascular compression disorder in which acute angulation of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) results in compression of the third part of the duodenum, leading to obstruction. Patients with SMA syndrome may present acutely, with chronic insidious symptomatology, or with an acute exacerbation of chronic symptoms: Acute presentation is usually characterised by signs and symptoms of duodenal obstruction Chronic cases may present with long-standing vague abdominal symptoms, early satiety and anorexia, or recurrent episodes of abdominal pain, associated with vomiting.
• Medical management - including decompression of the stomach and duodenum with a nasogastric tube , correction of nutritional and electrolytes deficiencies , through TPN , or preferably, if possible, enteral feeding with a naso-jejunal tube past the point of compression, which fulfils nutritional requirements while avoiding the complications of TPN. Surgical management - Duodenojejunostomy.
explanation.ref: Online reference https://radiopaedia.org/articles/superior-mesenteric-artery syndrome#:~:text=Superior%20mesenteric%20artery%20(SMA)%20syndrome,part%20of%20the%20duodenum%2C%20leading

subject: Anesthesia
question: All of these are a part of Mackler’s triad except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Pain . Pain, especially sudden severe chest pain , is a key feature of Mackler’s triad . It is typically retrosternal , exacerbated by swallowing , and may radiate to the back.
• Option C: Vomiting . Vomiting is a part of Mackler’s triad and is often the precipitating event leading to esophageal rupture in Boerhaave’s syndrome .
• Option D: Subcutaneous emphysema . Subcutaneous emphysema, which is air present in the subcutaneous tissue , can occur as a result of esophageal perforation with air escaping into the surrounding tissue . It is the third component of Mackler's triad.
• The correct answer to the question is Option B: Fever , as it is not part of Mackler’s triad.
• Educational Objective:
• Mackler’s triad , indicative of Boerhaave’s syndrome , includes vomiting, pain, and subcutaneous emphysema , but not fever .
• Spontaneous emetogenic perforation (Boerhaave’s syndrome) results from a sudden increase in oesophageal pressure against a closed glottis from vomiting. Mackler’s triad is a feature of Boerhaave’s syndrome.
• Typically, the site of perforation is the lower oesophagus towards the left pleural cavity . Hamman’s sign refers to a crunching sound on auscultation of the heart owing to surgical emphysema. Because of high septic load, urgent surgical intervention is usually required.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love's Short Practice of Surgery 28th Edition pg 1127

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these is incorrect about caustic injury to upper gastro intestinal tract?
options: Zargar’s grading is used for severity of injury
Multiple esophageal ulcers warrant urgent cervical esophagostomy

explanation.short: Ans. D) Multiple esophageal ulcers warrant urgent cervical esophagostomy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Alkali ingestion is more dangerous . Ingestion of alkali substances is indeed more dangerous compared to acids because alkalis cause liquefactive necrosis , allowing deeper tissue penetration and damage which can lead to more severe complications such as perforation.
• Option B: Zargar’s grading is used for severity of injury . Zargar’s grading system is a well-established scale used to classify the severity of caustic injuries to the esophagus based on endoscopic findings , helping to guide treatment decisions.
• Option C: It is a premalignant condition . Chronic injury from caustic ingestion can lead to esophageal strictures , which are a known risk factor for the development of esophageal cancer , thus making it a premalignant condition.
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of caustic injuries to the upper gastrointestinal tract , while alkali ingestion is indeed more dangerous, and Zargar’s grading is used to assess the severity of injury , and such injuries can be premalignant , the presence of multiple esophageal ulcers does not universally warrant an urgent cervical esophagostomy.
• The substance ingested can be a strong acid, causing coagulative necrosis with eschar formation , which may limit penetration to deeper layers, or strong alkali, leading to liquefactive necrosis . The latter potentially penetrates deeper into the oesophageal wall, producing a more severe injury pattern. It is a premalignant condition - Delayed complications include stricture and malignancy. Treatment - Most caustic injuries can be managed conservatively with supportive measures. Deterioration requires surgical treatment, with emergency esophagectomy. The oesophagus can be mobilised transhiatally or via a thoracoscopic approach. Immediate reconstruction is not recommended . A cervical oesophagostomy and a gastrostomy can be done and future reconstruction planned. A feeding jejunostomy is an alternative for nutritional support if the stomach also requires resection.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love's Short Practice of Surgery 28th Edition pg 1129

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these food groups is avoided in patients with irritable bowel syndrome?

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of the following is a true statement(s) about capsule endoscopy? It involves swallowing a small camera capsule that takes pictures of the digestive tract. It is used to diagnose conditions such as Crohn's disease and small intestine tumors. The capsule is disposable and does not need to be retrieved after the procedure. It is an invasive procedure that requires sedation.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It involves swallowing a small camera capsule that takes pictures of the digestive tract - This is a true statement about capsule endoscopy. The camera capsule is about the size of a large vitamin pill and contains a tiny camera and light source. The patient swallows the capsule, which then takes pictures of the digestive tract as it passes through the body. It is used to diagnose conditions such as Crohn's disease and small intestine tumors - This is also a true statement about capsule endoscopy. The procedure is typically used to diagnose conditions that affect the small intestine, such as Crohn's disease, celiac disease, and small intestine tumors. The capsule is disposable and does not need to be retrieved after the procedure - This is another true statement about capsule endoscopy. The camera capsule is disposable and does not need to be retrieved after the procedure. It is passed out of the body in the stool and can be safely disposed of. It is an invasive procedure that requires sedation - This statement is not true. Capsule endoscopy is a non-invasive procedure that does not require sedation . The patient can go about their normal activities during the procedure, although they may be asked to avoid certain foods and medications before and after the procedure.
• Educational Objective:
• Capsule endoscopy is a non-invasive diagnostic procedure that involves swallowing a small camera to take images of the digestive tract . It is used to diagnose conditions such as Crohn's disease and small intestine tumors , and the capsule is disposable , exiting the body naturally without the need for retrieval or sedation .
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28th Ed. Pg 153

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of the following statements is true regarding hemobilia? It is a rare condition where blood flows into the biliary tree. It is commonly caused by gallstones or inflammation of the gallbladder. Symptoms may include abdominal pain, jaundice, and Malena. Diagnosis is usually made through a liver biopsy.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is a rare condition where blood flows into the biliary tree - This statement is true. Hemobilia is a rare condition where bleeding occurs into the biliary tree . It is commonly caused by gallstones or inflammation of the gallbladder - This statement is not true. While gallstones or inflammation of the gallbladder can cause bleeding into the biliary tree, hemobilia is typically caused by trauma, surgery, or invasive procedures involving the liver or biliary tree. Symptoms of hemobilia may include abdominal pain, jaundice, and Malena (Sandbloom’s triad). Diagnosis is usually made through a liver biopsy - This statement is not entirely true . While a liver biopsy may be used to diagnose hemobilia in some cases, diagnosis is typically made through imaging tests like CECT abdomen .
• Educational Objective:
• Hemobilia is a rare condition characterized by bleeding into the biliary tract and is often associated with trauma or iatrogenic injury rather than gallstones or gallbladder inflammation . It typically presents with abdominal pain, jaundice, and melena , and the diagnosis is usually made through imaging and endoscopic procedures , not liver biopsy.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28th Ed. Pg 1202.

subject: Anesthesia
question: What is the role of Indocyanine green (ICG) dye during laparoscopic cholecystectomy?
options: To evaluate the function of the gallbladder
To identify biliary anatomy and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures
To minimize bleeding during surgery by highlighting blood vessels
To reduce the risk of surgical site infection

explanation.short: Ans. B) To identify biliary anatomy and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: To identify biliary anatomy and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures . This is the primary use of ICG during laparoscopic cholecystectomy. ICG binds to plasma proteins and is excreted into the bile , providing real-time cholangiography when viewed under near-infrared light , thus enhancing the visualization of the cystic duct, common bile duct, and other biliary structures to prevent injury.
• Option C: To minimize bleeding during surgery by highlighting blood vessels . ICG does help to visualize blood vessels; however, its use in laparoscopic cholecystectomy is more focused on biliary tract visualization rather than to minimize bleeding.
• Option D: To reduce the risk of surgical site infection . ICG has no antimicrobial properties and does not reduce the risk of surgical site infections .
• Educational Objective:
• The primary role of Indocyanine green (ICG) dye during laparoscopic cholecystectomy is to enhance the visualization of biliary anatomy , such as the cystic duct and common bile duct , in order to reduce the risk of injury to these and other surrounding structures.
• Indocyanine green (ICG) dye is used during laparoscopic cholecystectomy to help identify biliary anatomy, such as the common bile duct and cystic duct, and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures. ICG dye can also help to highlight blood vessels, which can aid in minimizing bleeding during surgery. However, this is not the main application in cholecystectomy. While ICG dye can be used to evaluate the function of the gallbladder and assess blood flow during surgery, this is not its primary role in laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28th Ed. Pg 173

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these is the ideal donor for organ transplant?
options: Brain dead donor who is an identical twin
Living donor who is an identical twin
Brain dead donor who has the same blood group
Living donor who has the same blood group

explanation.short: Ans. B) Living donor who is an identical twin
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. A brain-dead donor who is an identical twin would be an excellent match in terms of HLA compatibility . However, the organs may have suffered from the events leading to brain death and subsequent changes in physiology.
• Option C . A brain-dead donor who has the same blood group can be a suitable donor , but without the genetic matching of an identical twin , there is a higher risk of rejection .
• Option D . A living donor who has the same blood group is better than a deceased donor in terms of organ quality, but the risk of rejection would still be higher compared to an identical twin.
• Educational Objective:
• The ideal organ donor is a living donor who is an identical twin of the recipient due to the perfect HLA match and the optimal condition of the donated organ , which significantly reduces the risk of rejection and improves transplant outcomes .
• Transplants from live donors have a number of advantages over deceased donor organs . Live donor organ from healthy individuals without the comorbidities commonly seen in deceased donors . In the agonal period , before death , deceased donors exhibit marked changes in physiology related to a catecholamine storm and this can cause organ dysfunction . Clearly, live donor organs are not subjected to this insult. Allograft rejection is directed against human leukocyte antigens (HLAs) . These are a group of cell surface glycoprotein molecules. HLA molecules are divided into class I (A, B and C) and class II (DR, DP and DQ). They are highly polymorphic , i.e., their amino acid sequences differ widely between individuals . To give an example, there are > 1000 variants of the HLA-B gene . This genetic variability means that most transplant donors and recipients have different HLA profiles . HLA profiles will be the same in identical twins, hence chance of mismatch is low.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1597-99

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 55-year-old male has recently undergone a successful kidney transplant. To prevent graft rejection, his medical team plans to initiate maintenance immunosuppression therapy. Which of the following drugs is a mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) inhibitor that is suitable for this purpose?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Tacrolimus is a calcineurin inhibitor , not an mTOR inhibitor, and is used for immunosuppression in transplant patients , but it works via a different mechanism.
• Option C . Azathioprine is an antiproliferative agent and is used as an immunosuppressant ; however, it does not inhibit mTOR.
• Option D . Mycophenolate mofetil is also an antiproliferative agent that inhibits the proliferation of T and B cells but is not an mTOR inhibitor.
• Educational Objective:
• Sirolimus is a mammalian target of rapamycin ( mTOR ) inhibitor used in the maintenance phase of immunosuppression following kidney transplantation to prevent graft rejection .
• Drugs used for immunosuppression in a patient for kidney transplant are:
• Mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) inhibitors
• Immunosuppression for transplantation has two phases: Induction and maintenance . Induction therapy commonly consists of a combination of high-dose intravenous steroids and the anti-CD25 monoclonal antibody basiliximab, which blocks IL-2 receptors . Other induction agents for high-risk cases are ATG and the monoclonal antibody alemtuzumab.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1601-02

subject: Anesthesia
question: Identify the incorrect statement of the following about the technique of renal transplant?
options: The donor kidney is transplanted heterotopically into one of the iliac fossae
The transplant renal vein is anastomosed end to side to the external or common iliac vein.
The renal artery is anastomosed end to side to the external iliac artery in a deceased donor
Donor ureter is anastomosed to recipient ureter via a spatulated anastomosis

explanation.short: Ans. D) Donor ureter is anastomosed to recipient ureter via a spatulated anastomosis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. This is a correct statement as the donor kidney is typically placed in a location different from the original kidneys , which is in the retroperitoneal iliac fossa .
• Option B . This is the standard practice for the venous anastomosis in renal transplantation , making this statement correct .
• Option C . This is also correct as the renal artery can be anastomosed to the external iliac artery via a “Carrel patch” which can be obtained in deceased donor . In living donor transplants, the donor renal artery is anastomosed to recipient internal iliac artery .
• Educational Objective:
• In renal transplantation , the donor ureter is not anastomosed to the recipient ureter but is instead anastomosed to the bladder as an extra-vesical anastomosis using the Lich–Grégoir technique .
• Renal transplant operative technique
• The donor kidney is transplanted heterotopically into one of the iliac fossa via a curvilinear incision. The transplant renal vein is anastomosed end to side to the external or common iliac vein. The renal artery is anastomosed either end to side to the external or common iliac artery (via Carrels aortic patch) or end to end to the divided internal iliac artery The internal iliac artery is used more commonly for live donor kidneys because of the lack of an aortic patch. After revascularisation of the transplant kidney the ureter is anastomosed to the bladder as an extra-vesical only (the Lich–Grégoir technique)
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1604

subject: Anesthesia
question: Transplantation of a pair of marginal quality kidneys from the same donor into one recipient in order to provide adequate nephron mass is called as?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sub-optimal kidney transplant typically refers to a transplant involving a kidney that is not in optimal condition, but can be used a bridge to formal renal transplant .
• Option C . Additional kidney transplant is not a standard term used in transplant medicine for this scenario.
• Option D . Domino kidney transplant refers to a specific type of transplant where an organ or tissue is transplanted from one person to another and then from that person to another recipient , which is not the case described.
• Educational Objective:
• Dual kidney transplantation is the process where two marginal quality kidneys from the same donor are transplanted into one recipient to ensure adequate nephron mass and function .
• This involves the transplantation of a pair of marginal quality kidneys from the same donor into one recipient in order to provide adequate nephron mass. Both kidneys can be placed in the same iliac fossa . This approach is used for kidneys from elderly Donor after Circulatory Death and so-called expanded criteria donors, which are defined by age > 60 years or age >50 years with at least two of the following: hypertension; terminal creatinine >133 μmol/L; death from stroke.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1604

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these is not a cause of early graft dysfunction in renal transplant patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B : Renal vein thrombosis is a significant cause of early graft dysfunction due to the immediate impairment of blood flow it can cause in the transplanted kidney .
• Option C : Renal artery stenosis can lead to early graft dysfunction by reducing blood flow to the transplanted kidney , causing ischemia and potentially acute renal failure .
• Option D : Dehydration can cause pre-renal acute kidney injury by decreasing blood flow to the kidneys , leading to early graft dysfunction if severe .
• Educational Objective:
• Recurrence of the original disease is not a common cause of early graft dysfunction in renal transplant patients; as such recurrences typically occur later in the post-transplant course . Early graft dysfunction is more often caused by factors such as acute rejection, vascular complications, infections, or technical issues .
• Causes of early graft dysfunction
• Any rise in serum creatinine of >10% of baseline or ≥20 μmol/L should be considered as acute allograft dysfunction that requires investigation . Possible causes are:
• Acute Rejection (Antibody Mediated or Cell Mediated) Calcineurin Inhibitor Toxicity Dehydration Urinary Tract Infection or Pyelonephritis Any other source of Sepsis Renal Vein or Renal Artery Thrombosis Ureteric Obstruction or Urine Leak
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1606

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 58-year-old male who underwent a kidney transplant 7 years ago presents with gradually worsening renal function over the past year. He has no signs of infection, maintains good hydration, and reports compliance with his immunosuppressive medications. A renal biopsy reveals interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy without evidence of acute inflammatory changes. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's renal dysfunction?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acute rejection, both antibody-mediated (AMR) and cell-mediated (CMR) , can lead to early graft dysfunction (within few months).
• Option C . Renal artery stenosis can lead to graft dysfunction due to ischemia ; however, it is less common than chronic rejection and doesn’t match the biopsy.
• Option D . Dehydration can cause acute pre-renal failure but is not a common cause of long-term graft failure in kidney transplant patients.
• Educational Objective:
• Chronic rejection is the most common cause of graft failure/dysfunction in kidney transplant , typically presenting as progressive organ dysfunction over time due to immunological factors .
• Causes of long-term graft dysfunction
• Chronic AMR (anti-body mediated rejection) Acute CMR (cell mediated rejection) or AMR, which can occur at any time
• Pre-existing damage in the donor kidney (especially relevant to DCD and extended criteria donor kidneys) Early ischaemia - Reperfusion injury Chronic calcineurin nephrotoxicity Ureteric or bladder outflow obstruction Recurrent urinary tract infection or pyelonephritis BK polyomavirus nephropathy Recurrent native disease: glomerulonephritis; focal segmental glomerulosclerosis; immunoglobulin A nephropathy Renal artery stenosis Poorly controlled hypertension Dyslipidaemia
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1606-1607

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 63-year-old male, who received a kidney transplant 2 years ago and has been on chronic immunosuppressive therapy, presents with multiple hyperkeratotic lesions on sun-exposed areas of the skin. Biopsy of one lesion confirms malignancy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Skin

explanation.short: Ans. B) Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Skin
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . PTLD refers to a range of lymphoid proliferations that can occur following transplantation due to immunosuppression . However, it does not present with hyperkeratotic skin lesions.
• Option C . Leukemia can occur in the post-transplant setting but would not present with localized skin lesions, but with pancytopenia and splenomegaly.
• Option D . Kaposi’s sarcoma can occur in transplant patients but typically presents as violaceous plaques or nodules and is less common than squamous cell carcinoma in these patients.
• Educational Objective:
• The most common malignancy seen post-transplant in patients on immunosuppressive therapy is squamous cell carcinoma of the skin , often occurring on sun-exposed areas .
• Most common malignancy seen post-transplant immunosuppressive therapy is Squamous cell carcinoma of skin
• 1. CMV - 4 to 8 weeks Post-Transplant - Pneumonia/Retinitis/Encephalitis
• 2. Pneumocystis jirovecii - Fungal Pneumonia
• Malignancy: - Skin Cancers (MC- Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love 27 th Ed. Pg 1541, Box 82.5

subject: Anesthesia
question: Maastricht grading is used to categorize?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Deceased Circulatory Dead Donor

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 46-year-old male with a history of a brain lesion treated with surgical resection and no evidence of metastasis is evaluated for kidney transplantation. Which of the following conditions in the patient's history is not considered an absolute contraindication to transplant?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Severe acute pancreatitis with sepsis is typically a contraindication , until the sepsis is under control . Active systemic sepsis at the time of transplant would be an absolute contraindication .
• Option B . Melanoma is an absolute contraindication to transplant . Non-melanotic skin tumors are not.
• Option C. Prion disease , such as Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease , is an absolute contraindication to organ transplantation due to the high risk of transmitting the disease through transplanted tissues .
• Educational Objective:
• Primary CNS tumors, particularly those that are localized and have been treated without evidence of metastasis, are not considered an absolute contraindication to organ transplantation. This is in contrast to systemic malignancies and certain untreatable or high-risk infections.
• Creutzfeldt Jacobson Disease (Prion Disease) Active Systemic Sepsis Malignancy in previous 5 years (except Primary CNS tumour, Non-Melanotic Skin Ca and CIS of uterus)
• As a further response to the organ donor shortage , organs are now being transplanted from donors with meningitis/encephalitis , human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) , hepatitis B and C and high-risk behaviour with the potential for blood-borne infection
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1596-97

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 25-year-old type 1 diabetic with ESRD underwent simultaneous pancreatic-kidney transplant (SPK). Which of these is not a complication of bladder drainage of transplanted pancreas?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• If transplanted pancreas is drained into bladder instead of small bowel , there is loss of bicarbonates from the pancreatic secretions , leading to metabolic acidosis .
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1627

subject: Anesthesia
question: A 55-year-old patient with Child C Cirrhosis and Hepatitis C is undergoing a liver transplant. Which anatomical structure should the surgeon prepare to anastomose first in the recipient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D : The IVC, both supra-hepatic and infra-hepatic , is usually the first part to be anastomosed during liver transplantation to establish venous outflow from the liver , which is critical to providing immediate blood drainage from the graft .
• Educational Objective
• The first structure to be anastomosed in the recipient of a liver transplant is the IVC , specifically starting with the supra-hepatic IVC , followed by the infra-hepatic IVC , to establish adequate venous outflow from the liver .
• Order of anastomosis in recipient of liver transplant is:
• Supra-hepatic IVC Infra-hepatic IVC Portal Vein Hepatic Artery Bile duct
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1610-1611

subject: Anesthesia
question: In a patient with graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) following a hematopoietic stem cell transplant, which organ is least likely to affect by this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The skin is commonly affected in GVHD, presenting with rashes and can progress to desquamation .
• Option B. The gastrointestinal tract is frequently involved in GVHD, which can manifest as diarrhea, abdominal pain , and gastrointestinal bleeding .
• Option C. The liver can be affected in GVHD, leading to elevated liver enzymes , jaundice , and cholestasis .
• Educational objective:
• While any organ can be affected by graft-versus-host disease , the lung is typically the least affected organ compared to the skin, liver , and gastrointestinal tract , which are more commonly involved in acute presentations of the disease .
• Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) occurs due to the presence of immunocompetent T lymphocytes in the graft attacking the immunodeficient recipient tissue due to histocompatibility differences within 100 days , causing tissue damage. Organ most affected by GVHD - Skin Organ least affected by GVHD - Lung Any organ can be affected in GVH reaction. The most important is the involvement of immune system, skin, liver and intestines.
explanation.ref: Online reference – Bailey and Love 27 th Ed. Pg 1537 .

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these is incorrect about liver transplant?
options: Most common indication in a child is biliary atresia
Liver transplant may be done in liver malignancies
In auxiliary liver transplant, native liver is left in situ
Liver is resistant to acute rejection

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Biliary atresia is indeed the most common indication for liver transplantation in children , making this statement correct.
• Option B . Liver transplantation is an established treatment for selected patients with Hepato-Cellular Carcinoma (HCC) and fulfills specific criteria such as the Milan or UCSF criteria .
• Option C. In auxiliary liver transplantation, part of the native liver is left in place, and a portion of a donor liver is implanted . This procedure may be considered for certain indications, making this statement correct.
• Educational objective:
• The liver is not resistant to acute rejection ; it is susceptible to acute rejection like any other transplanted organ, and acute cellular rejection is a common cause of graft dysfunction early after liver transplantation .
• In adults the most common causes are alcoholic liver disease, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), chronic viral hepatitis (hepatitis B virus [HBV] and hepatitis C virus [HCV]) . In the last two decades hepatitis-related CLD (HBV and HCV) was the most common indication for LT. In children, who account for around 10–15% of all LTs, biliary atresia is the most common indication for transplantation. LTx is indicated as a curative treatment for selected patients with HCC , haemangioendothelioma and hepatoblastoma. Milan criteria (one lesion ≤5 cm, or three or fewer lesions ≤3 cm each) UCSF criteria (one lesion ≤6.5 cm, or three or fewer lesions ≤4.5 cm each, with a total tumour diameter ≤8 cm) Auxiliary LT involves implanting a healthy liver graft placed either heterotopically or orthotopically while leaving all or part of the native liver intact. Auxiliary heterotopic LT, where the graft is implanted below the native liver, was proposed as an alternative to orthotopic LT. Liver graft dysfunction can happen any time after transplantation; if not identified early and treated promptly, it can lead to graft loss. The most common presentation is an asymptomatic elevation of liver enzyme levels . Early after LT, acute cellular rejection is the most common cause of graft dysfunction and is usually treated by increasing the dose of immunosuppression.

subject: Anesthesia
question: Identify the incorrect statement about graft rejection:
options: Hyperacute Rejection occurs due to T Cell Mediated Destruction
Acute Rejection occurs during first 6 Months
Chronic Rejection is the most common cause of Graft Failure
Chronic Rejection is characterized by Myo-Intimal Proliferation on Biopsy

explanation.short: Ans. A) Hyperacute Rejection occurs due to T Cell Mediated Destruction
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This statement is generally accurate . Acute rejection is most common in the early post-transplant period , and it often occurs within the first few months after transplantation . However, acute rejection can occur at any time post-transplant .
• This statement is correct . Chronic rejection can contribute to graft failure over time. Graft failure can result from various factors, including acute rejection , infections , vascular complications , and other non-immunological factors.
• This statement is generally accurate . Chronic rejection is associated with long-term changes , including fibrosis and myo-intimal proliferation in the blood vessels of the transplanted organ . This can be observed on biopsy as a characteristic feature of chronic rejection .
• Educational Objective:
• Types of Graft Rejection
• Hyperacute rejection Immediate graft destruction due to ABO or preformed anti-HLA antibodies Characterised by intravascular thrombosis and interstitial haemorrhage Acute rejection Usually occurs during first 6 months T-cell dependent May be cell mediated, antibody mediated or both Usually reversible Chronic rejection Occurs after first 6 months Most common cause of graft failure Antibodies play an important role Noe-immune factors contribute to pathogenesis Characterised by myointimal proliferation in graft arteries leading to ischaemia and fibrosis
explanation.ref: Bailey 27 th Ed. PG 1535.

subject: Anesthesia
question:
A 22-year-old female is posted for open reduction internal fixation of fracture of right humerus shaft under general anaesthesia. One hour into the procedure this is her capnograph. What could not be the possible diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/12/15/picture117.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The above capnograph shows sudden fall in etCO 2 .
• Causes of sudden fall in etCO 2 :
• Circuit disconnection Accidental extubation Cardiac arrest Obstruction in endotracheal tube or circuit
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: - Dorsch and Dorsch anesthesia equipment, 5 th ed, pg 1023-1024

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these devices is not used intra-operatively for cardiac output monitoring?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Thermodilution : This is a technique used to measure cardiac output . A cold solution is injected into the right atrium , and as it travels through the heart , a temperature sensor in a pulmonary artery catheter measures the change in blood temperature . The amount of dilution correlates with cardiac output . Thus, this technique is used intra-operatively for cardiac output monitoring .
• Option C . Pulse contour device : This device measures cardiac output based on the shape of the arterial waveform . It uses the principle that the contour (shape) of the arterial pulse waveform is related to the stroke volume and thus can give a measure of cardiac output . They are used intra-operatively , especially when invasive measures like a pulmonary artery catheter are deemed unnecessary .
• Option D . Thoracic bioimpedance : This method measures cardiac output by sending a small electrical current across the thorax and measuring the resultant voltage . The idea is that blood is a good conductor , and as the heart pumps blood (i.e., as cardiac output changes ), the impedance or resistance to the current in the thorax changes . While it can be used in some settings, it's less commonly used intra-operatively because factors like surgical manipulation, fluid shifts, and other interventions can affect thoracic impedance, making it less reliable than the other methods in the operating room setting .
• Educational Objective
• Devices used intra-operatively for cardiac output monitoring:
• Thermodilution Pulse Contour Device Thoracic Bioimpedance Transesophageal Echo Esophageal Doppler
• :- Morgan and Mikhail’s clinical anaesthesiology 5 th ed pg 87-109
explanation.ref: - Morgan and Mikhail’s clinical anaesthesiology 5 th ed pg 87-109

subject: Anesthesia
question:
A 36-year-old female is posted for Modified radical mastectomy under general anesthesia. Halfway through the surgery, this is her capnograph. What should be the next step?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/25/cerebellum-anesthesia-mcqs-06.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Patient cannot be extubated as they are halfway through the surgery.
• Option C: Deepening anaesthesia will not paralyze the patient.
• Option D: Patient cannot be reversed from neuromuscular blockade as they are halfway through the surgery , surgery is not over.
• Educational Objective:
• In cases where the capnograph waveform shows a curare cleft in the capnograph during surgery , the appropriate next step may be to repeat the muscle relaxant to ensure adequate muscle paralysis for the procedure.
• A) It is the surest sign of Intubation
• B) To diagnose a venous air embolism
• C) To diagnose any circuit disconnections
• D) To identify Bronchospasm intra-operatively
• E)To diagnose Malignant Hyperthermia and exhausted soda lime
• F) To monitor the performance of chest compressions in CPR
explanation.ref: - Dorsch and Dorsch anesthesia equipment, 5 th ed, pg 1016

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these arteries is best preferred for invasive blood pressure monitoring?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The Radial artery is the preferred site for invasive blood pressure monitoring for several reasons:
• Accessibility : The radial artery is superficial and is easily accessible . This makes cannulation of the radial artery relatively straightforward . Collateral Blood Flow : The hand has a dual blood supply , with both the ulnar and radial arteries contributing. This means that if the radial artery becomes occluded, the hand still has a blood supply from the ulnar artery. Before cannulation , the Allen's test can be done to ensure good ulnar artery perfusion . Stable Signal : The radial artery provides a stable and clear pressure waveform , which is essential for accurate monitoring . Low Complication Rate : Complications such as infection , thrombosis, or embolism are relatively lower in the radial artery compared to other sites. Patient Comfort : The wrist's position can be more comfortable for the patient compared to other potential sites for arterial line placement. Easy to Dress and Monitor : Because of its location, the site is easier for healthcare providers to dress, monitor, and manage , reducing the risk of complications .
• Image showing Modified Allen Test – to check the patency of Ulnar Palmar Arch
• Other arteries used for invasive blood pressure monitoring :
• Dorsalis Pedis, Posterior Tibial, Superficial Temporal, Ulnar, Brachial, Femoral, and Axillary .
• Educational Objective
• The radial artery is the preferred choice for invasive blood pressure monitoring for several reasons, including accessibility , collateral blood flow , stability of the pressure waveform , low complication rate , patient comfort , and ease of dressing and monitoring .
• Advantages of Intra-arterial Blood Pressure monitoring:
• 1) Beat to Beat Monitoring which is essential for major surgeries like CABG, Transplant surgeries
• 2) For repeated ABG blood sampling
explanation.ref: - Millers anesthesia 8 th ed pg 1349-1350.

subject: Anesthesia
question:
Identify the gauge of the IV cannula?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/25/cerebellum-anesthesia-mcqs-31.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Size , Flow Rate and Colour of IV Cannula
• Color-coding of IV cannulas
• Educational Objective:
• Orange  intravenous (IV) cannula is a 14-gauge (Option D) IV cannula
explanation.ref: - Equipment in anesthesia and critical care pg 264

subject: Anesthesia
question: A patient is undergoing neurosurgery under general anaesthesia. Before extubation the anaesthetist wants to check recovery from neuromuscular blockade. Which of these nerves is most commonly monitored for the same?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The Ulnar nerve is most commonly monitored for assessing recovery from neuromuscular blockade for several reasons:
• Consistency in Responses : The adductor pollicis muscle , innervated by the ulnar nerve , is more sensitive to neuromuscular blocking agents than other muscles. This makes it a good choice for monitoring neuromuscular blockade and ensuring complete reversalbefore extubation . Ease of Monitoring : The ulnar nerve is easily accessible at the wrist , and the thumb's movement (adduction) against resistance can be readily observed and felt . The setup for monitoring using a peripheral nerve stimulator is straightforward. Minimal Interference : Monitoring at the ulnar nerve has minimal interference from surrounding muscles and structures. This provides a clear assessment of the blockade's depth and recovery . Reliable Predictive Value : The degree of neuromuscular blockade at the adductor pollicis muscle ( innervated by the ulnar nerve ) is more indicative of blockade in the diaphragm and laryngeal muscles , which are critical for effective breathing and airway protection post-extubation . While other nerves can also be used for monitoring neuromuscular blockade, the ulnar nerve's advantages make it the preferred choice in many clinical scenarios, especially for ensuring safe extubation in neurosurgery patients .
• Other nerves that can be monitored:
• Facial nerve – Look for twitch of orbicularis oculi (eye) Posterior tibial nerve – look for twitch of flexor hallucis brevis muscle
• Educational Objective
• Ulnar nerve is the most commonly monitored nerve to assess recovery from neuromuscular blockade
explanation.ref: - Millers anesthesia 8 th edition pg 1610

subject: Anesthesia
question:
A patient on antipsychotics is posted for laparoscopic appendectomy under general anesthesia with halothane. 20 minutes into the procedure his temperature rises to 41 degrees Celsius and this is his capnograph. Which of these cannot be a possible explanation of the same?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/25/cerebellum-anesthesia-mcqs-07.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The displayed capnograph demonstrates an elevated end-tidal carbon dioxide (ETCO 2 ) level , which is characteristic of certain conditions that can result in increased metabolic activity or decreased removal of carbon dioxide .
• Given the clinical context and options provided:
• Option A . Malignant Hyperthermia (MH) : MH is a life-threatening reaction to certain anesthetic agents, like halothane, in genetically susceptible individuals . It's characterized by rapid elevation of body temperature , tachycardia, muscle rigidity , and increased ETCO 2 . This is a possible explanation for the scenario described.
• Option B . Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS) : This is a life-threatening reaction to antipsychotic medications . Symptoms include hyperthermia, altered mental status, muscle rigidity, and autonomic dysfunction . Elevated ETCO 2 may be observed due to muscle rigidity and reduced respiratory effort . This too could explain the scenario.
• Option D . Thyrotoxicosis : This is an excessive release of thyroid hormones , which can result in increased metabolic rate and heat production . However, the rapid onset of symptoms as described in the scenario is less typical for thyrotoxicosis, but it can't be completely ruled out based on the provided information.
• Thus, based on the provided information and capnograph , the least likely explanation is: Option C. Hyperventilation.
• D/D of rise in etCO2:
• Malignant hyperthermia Neuroleptic malignant syndrome Hypoventilation Thyrotoxicosis Fever, pain Shivering
explanation.ref: - Millers anesthesia 9 th edition pg 1313

subject: Anesthesia
question:
A patient is posted for tracheal resection surgery under general anesthesia with halothane. Immediately after intubation this is his capnograph. Which of these is a possible explanation for the same?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/25/cerebellum-anesthesia-mcqs-08.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The displayed capnograph shows a flat or baseline tracing , indicating the absence of detectable end-tidal carbon dioxide (ETCO 2 ) throughout the respiratory cycle .
• Given the clinical context and options provided:
• Option A . Malignant Hyperthermia (MH): MH typically shows an elevated ETCO 2 due to increased metabolic activity . This would not produce a flat capnograph .
• Option B . Hypoventilation : While hypoventilation would lead to reduced ventilation and possibly elevated ETCO 2 , it would not lead to a completely flat capnograph as the one displayed.
• Option D . Endobronchial Intubation : This is when the endotracheal tube is advanced too far and goes into one of the main bronchi , typically the right main bronchus . This would lead to the ventilation of only one lung , but ETCO 2 would still be detectable , even if altered. The capnograph would not be completely flat
• Given the capnograph and the provided information , the most likely explanation is:
• Option C. Esophageal Intubation .
• Educational Objective:
• D/D of fall in etCO2:
• Hypothermia Hypotension Acidosis Hyperventilation Circuit disconnection Esophageal intubation Pulmonary embolism
explanation.ref: - Millers anesthesia 9 th edition pg 1313

subject: Anesthesia
question: Which of these monitors can help in measuring the Right atrial pressure and the pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Central Venous Cannulation :
• It primarily measures the Central Venous Pressure (CVP), which reflects the right atrial pressure . It does not measure the Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure (PCWP) , which provides an indirect estimate of the left atrial pressure.
• Option C. Transesophageal Echo (TEE) :
• This involves placing an ultrasound probe in the esophagus to obtain images of the heart . TEE can provide valuable hemodynamic information , including assessments of ventricular function, valvular function , and more. Indirect measurements or estimates of right atrial pressure and left atrial pressure can be made using Doppler principles , but it doesn't directly measure pressures like a catheter would.
• Option D. Transthoracic Echo (TTE):
• This is a standard echocardiographic method where an ultrasound probe is placed on the chest wall . Like TEE, TTE provides visual and Doppler assessments of cardiac structures and function . Right atrial pressure can be indirectly estimated from the inferior vena cava's size and collapsibility . While it provides valuable information about cardiac function and structure , it does not directly measure the pressures .
• Educational Objective
• Also known as a Swan-Ganz catheter , it is introduced into the venous system and then advanced into the pulmonary artery . This catheter can measure right atrial pressure , right ventricular pressure , pulmonary artery pressure , and, when wedged, the PCWP . PCWP is especially useful as it gives an indirect measurement of left atrial pressure and can provide insights into left ventricular function . Therefore, this is the most direct and accurate method to measure both right atrial pressure and PCWP among the options listed.
explanation.ref: - Millers anesthesia 8 th ed pg 137

subject: Anesthesia
question: Maximum etCO 2 is measured at the end of which phase of the capnograph?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Phase I (Inspiratory Baseline) reflects inspired air , which is normally devoid of CO2 . Phase II (Expiratory Upstroke) reflects the dead space air ( trachea and conduction bronchi ) Phase III is the Alveolar Plateau . Phase IV/Phase 0 is the Inspiratory Downstroke , the beginning of the next inspiration . Maximum measured etCO 2 is seen at the end of phase III
• Educational Objective:
• The maximum end-tidal carbon dioxide ( etCO 2 ) measurement is observed at the end of Phase III in the capnograph waveform , which represents the alveolar plateau.
explanation.ref: - Dorsch and Dorsch anesthesia equipment, 5 th ed, pg. 1023-1024

subject: Anesthesia
question:
A 40-year-old chronic smoker was scheduled for robotic prostatectomy under general anesthesia. He was induced with propofol and vecuronium and started on isoflurane for maintenance of anesthesia. During the procedure, the following capnograph plot was observed. What is the possible cause?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/25/cerebellum-anesthesia-mcqs-10.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Let’s evaluate each option:
• Option A . Pulmonary Embolism :
• In the context of capnography, a pulmonary embolism would often lead to a sudden drop in the end-tidal CO 2 (ETCO 2 ) level . This is because the blood flow to the alveoli is impeded , leading to decreased carbon dioxide exchange . The capnograph for pulmonary embolism typically shows a sudden decrease in the height of the waveform .
• Option B. Leak from Expiratory Valve :
• A leak in the expiratory valve of the breathing system can cause a reduction in the measured ETCO 2 as some of the exhaled CO 2 might escapebefore it can be measured . The capnograph in such cases may show a reduced ETCO 2 or sometimes even a flattened baseline if there is a significant leak .
• Option D . Rebreathing :
• Rebreathing refers to the inhalation of previously exhaled gases . In the context of capnography, rebreathing often leads to an elevated baseline ( the CO 2 doesn't return to zero between breaths ) because CO 2 from the previous breath is being inhaled again .
• Given the capnograph and the provided options, Endobronchial Intubation (C) seems to be the most appropriate explanation for the observed pattern.
• Educational Objective
• This results in ventilation of one lung and collapse or reduced ventilation of the other. The capnograph often shows a 'shark-fin' appearance , with a gradual upstroke during inspiration followed by a rapid downstroke during expiration . This is because the unventilated lung leads to mixing of fresh gas with exhaled gas during inspiration.
• The provided capnograph seems to demonstrate this 'shark-fin' appearance, making endobronchial intubation a likely cause.
explanation.ref: - Millers anesthesia 9 th edition pg 1313-131

subject: Anesthesia
question:
The monitor shown below is used for:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/25/cerebellum-anesthesia-mcqs-34.jpg]
options: To know the potency of General Anesthesia
To know the speed of Induction
To monitor the depth of General Anesthesia
To monitor the depth of Neuromuscular Blockade

explanation.short: Ans. C) To monitor the depth of General Anesthesia
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The monitor shown above is a Bipsectral Index monitor .
• It is the first scientifically validated and commercially available monitor to check depth of anaesthesia . It utilizes many parameters like EEG signals, eye blinks etc. to calculate a score . The score varies between 0-100 . A score of 45-60 is considered as adequate depth (score of 100 is for fully awake state and 0 is for completely silent brain ).
• Let’s explain each option:
• Option A . To know the potency of General Anesthesia : Potency usually refers to the concentration or dose of an anesthetic agent required to produce a specific effect . While the BIS monitor can give insights into how the brain is responding to anesthesia, it doesn't directly measure the potency of the agent being used.
• Option B. To know the speed of Induction: The speed of induction refers to how quickly a patient becomes anesthetized after the administration of an anesthetic agent . While the BIS value can decrease as a patient becomes more anesthetized , the primary purpose of BIS monitoring isn't to measure this speed .
• Option D. To monitor the depth of Neuromuscular Blockade: Neuromuscular blockade is achieved using specific drugs to paralyze muscles during surgery . The depth of neuromuscular blockade is usually monitored using peripheral nerve stimulators and not with a BIS monitor
• Educational Objective
• The Bispectral Index Monitor, commonly referred to as the BIS monitor, is a valuable tool in anesthesia and critical care . It measures and quantifies a patient's level of consciousness and depth of anesthesia by analyzing EEG (electroencephalogram) patterns . The BIS monitor provides real-time feedback to anesthesiologists, helping them maintain an appropriate balance of anesthesia during surgical procedures and improve patient outcomes .
• :- Understanding anaesthetic equipment and procedures – Baheti, first ed pg 304
explanation.ref: - Understanding anaesthetic equipment and procedures – Baheti, first ed pg 304

subject: Anesthesia
question:
The most preferred site of insertion of the catheter shown below is right internal jugular vein. Which of these is not a reason for the same?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/03/25/cerebellum-anesthesia-mcqs-36.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. A. Direct communication with left ventricle
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
